classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Not only is it written in the Old Testament, "I make good, and create evil;" but more clearly in the New Testament, where the Lord says, "Fear not them which kill the body, and have no more that they can do; but fear him who, after he has killed the body, has power to cast the soul into hell."

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: And that to voluntary corruption penal corruption is added in the divine judgment, is plainly declared by the Apostle Paul, when he says, "The temple of God is holy, which temple ye are; whoever corrupts the temple of God, him will God corrupt."

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If this had been said in the Old Law, how vehemently would the Manichæans have denounced it as making God a corrupter!

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: And from fear of the word, many Latin translators make it, "him shall God destroy," instead of corrupt, avoiding the offensive word without any change of meaning.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Although these would inveigh against any passage in the Old Law or the prophets if God was called in it a destroyer.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: But the Greek original here shows that corrupt is the true word; for it is written distinctly, "Whoever corrupts the temple of God, him will God corrupt."

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If the Manichæans are asked to explain the words, they will say, to escape making God a corrupter, that corrupt here means to give over to corruption, or some such explanation.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Did they read the Old Law in this spirit, they would both find many admirable things in it; and instead of spitefully attacking passages which they did not understand, they would reverently postpone the inquiry.
